title: Network at a glance
text: As of Perigee's Q2 2026 network status report, the platform coordinates 44 leased ground stations spanning 19 countries across six continents, supporting S-band, X-band, and Ka-band telemetry, tracking, and command links. Perigee currently holds contracts with 215 satellite operators, collectively tracking roughly 1,900 active spacecraft, and schedules an average of 14,600 discrete contact passes per day network-wide. The platform runs version 6.4, released in April 2026, the tenth major release since the original 1.0 launch in March 2019. Contractual service levels guarantee 98.7% contact-slot fulfillment measured on a rolling 30-day basis, with penalty credits issued automatically whenever a region falls below that threshold for two consecutive billing cycles.

title: What Perigee does
text: Perigee brokers time on a shared network of leased ground-station antennas so that satellite operators who cannot justify building their own ground segment can still reach their spacecraft on a predictable schedule. Rather than owning dishes outright, an operator submits its orbital elements and a downlink plan, and Perigee's scheduling engine finds contact windows across whichever leased stations can see the satellite as it passes overhead. Two capabilities sit at the center of the platform: predicting exactly when and for how long a given station can see a given satellite, and resolving the inevitable conflicts that arise when several operators want the same antenna during the same few minutes. Because ground stations are a shared, finite resource, Perigee treats scheduling less like a calendar and more like an allocation problem, one it re-solves continuously as new requests, cancellations, and updated orbital data arrive throughout the day.

title: Operator onboarding checklist
text:
New satellite operators move through a fixed sequence before Perigee will accept their first scheduling request.
1. Submit two-line element sets or operator-supplied ephemeris for every spacecraft to be tracked, refreshed at least once per orbit.
2. Define a minimum elevation mask and priority tier for each mission, since both directly affect how contact requests are ranked during conflicts.
3. Complete an RF compatibility review confirming the spacecraft's transponder matches the frequency plan at each candidate ground station.
4. Sign the applicable station-lease schedule and countersign Perigee's contact-slot service agreement.
5. Execute a supervised test pass against a low-priority station to validate telemetry lock and Doppler-compensated downlink before production passes begin.
Operators who skip the RF compatibility review are the single most common cause of first-pass antenna lock failures reported to Perigee's operations desk.

title: Scheduling engine performance
text: Perigee's scheduling engine recomputes the full network allocation plan every 90 seconds, folding in newly submitted requests, cancellations, and refreshed orbital data since the previous cycle. During a December 2025 load test simulating 3,000 simultaneous contact requests across the entire network, worst-case replan latency measured 4.1 seconds, well inside the 10-second budget the engine is designed against. Average replan latency under normal daytime load sits closer to 0.6 seconds. The engine holds a rolling 72-hour planning horizon, discarding and regenerating schedule entries beyond that window rather than committing to long-range allocations that upstream orbital-data drift would likely invalidate. Peak simultaneous in-flight requests during the same load test reached 11,400 before any queuing delay became measurable at the API layer.

title: Per-station conflict resolution
text: Within a single ground station's timeline, two contact requests conflict only if their intervals overlap, so the conflict relationship forms an interval graph, and interval graphs are perfect: their chromatic number always equals the size of their largest clique. Perigee exploits this directly, coloring each station's request graph with a sweep-line greedy algorithm in O(n log n) rather than treating single-station allocation as a general resource-constrained scheduling problem, which is NP-hard in the worst case. If k is the largest number of simultaneously overlapping requests at a station and C is that station's simultaneous-channel capacity, typically C = 3 at a Perigee X-band site, the station can honor every request only if k <= C; otherwise the excess requests are pushed to the network-level allocator. That cross-station allocator, choosing which of several candidate stations serves a given pass, remains a genuinely NP-hard assignment problem and is handled with a bounded-lookahead heuristic instead.

title: Pass prediction and orbital data
text: Predicting a contact window starts with propagating each tracked spacecraft's orbital state forward using SGP4, producing an acquisition-of-signal and loss-of-signal time, a maximum elevation, and an azimuth track for every candidate ground station. Perigee ingests a combined-catalog TLE feed on a fixed refresh cycle and layers in operator-supplied ephemeris whenever an operator's own orbit-determination pipeline is more current than the public catalog. Prediction accuracy degrades gracefully as element sets age, which is why the scheduling engine treats a stale TLE as a soft warning rather than a hard failure at first, only escalating to a blocked schedule entry once staleness crosses a configurable threshold. A pass prediction that later proves wrong, most often because of an unannounced maneuver, is corrected on the next TLE refresh rather than requiring manual operator intervention, though the affected contact window is flagged for review if the predicted and observed acquisition times diverge by more than a few seconds.

title: Ground network footprint by region
text:
Perigee's 44 leased stations are unevenly distributed across regions, reflecting both customer demand and available lease partners, as the current footprint shows.

| Region | Stations | Bands supported | Avg. daily passes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nordic & Arctic | 9 | S, X, Ka | 3,100 |
| North America | 12 | S, X | 3,900 |
| Asia-Pacific | 11 | S, X, Ka | 4,200 |
| South America | 6 | S, X | 1,700 |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | 4 | S | 950 |
| Antarctica-adjacent | 2 | X, Ka | 750 |

Asia-Pacific and North America together account for over half of all scheduled passes, while the two Antarctica-adjacent stations, though few, see disproportionately high demand from polar-orbiting Earth-observation missions needing frequent high-latitude contacts.

title: Doppler compensation control loop
text: Perigee's Doppler compensation subsystem recalculates the predicted line-of-sight velocity between spacecraft and antenna every 200 milliseconds throughout an active pass, steering the ground station's receive and transmit local oscillators to track the shifting carrier frequency in real time. Measured against telemetry logs from the second quarter of 2026, residual frequency tracking error averages under 15 Hz RMS at X-band and under 40 Hz RMS at Ka-band, comfortably inside the lock tolerance of the narrowband demodulators most operators fly. The control loop's update rate was raised from 500 milliseconds to 200 milliseconds in the version 5.0 release, cutting residual tracking error roughly in half for high-inclination passes with steep Doppler curves. Stations still running firmware older than version 4.2 cap out at the earlier 500-millisecond update rate.

title: Elevation mask and link margin
text: Received signal-to-noise ratio during a pass follows SNR_dB = EIRP_dBW + G/T_dB - L_path_dB(epsilon) - 10*log10(k*B), where L_path_dB grows sharply as elevation angle epsilon approaches the horizon because the signal traverses a longer atmospheric path, roughly proportional to csc(epsilon). Perigee defaults every station to a 5-degree minimum elevation mask, epsilon_min = 5°, the point at which modeled atmospheric loss stays under about 1.2 dB for a typical X-band link, against roughly 4 dB or more below 2 degrees. A lower mask buys additional contact seconds at the start and end of every pass, but only a handful of high-gain missions request the 3-degree mask Perigee permits at its polar sites, since most transponders' link margin cannot absorb the added path loss without an unacceptable bit-error-rate increase near the horizon.

title: Antenna commissioning checklist
text:
Bringing a newly leased antenna into the Perigee network follows a fixed commissioning sequence before any operator traffic is routed to it.
1. Verify the station's pointing accuracy against a set of reference calibration passes using known, high-confidence orbital elements.
2. Characterize the station's G/T figure across its supported bands and load the measured values into the link-budget model.
3. Confirm the local oscillator's Doppler-tracking control loop locks within tolerance during a live test pass.
4. Integrate the station's scheduling API endpoint with Perigee's network-level allocator and run a 72-hour shadow-scheduling period without live traffic.
5. Promote the station to production capacity only after shadow-scheduling shows zero missed handoffs.
A station that fails the pointing-accuracy check is re-surveyed rather than commissioned on a provisional basis, regardless of schedule pressure from waiting operators.

title: Korean-language Doppler tracking research
text: Perigee's Doppler compensation control loop draws on tracking-rate research published well outside the English-language ground-segment literature most engineers read first. Kim and Park's 2019 paper, 저궤도 위성의 도플러 편이 보정을 위한 적응형 주파수 추적 기법, develops an adaptive frequency-tracking technique for low-Earth-orbit Doppler compensation, and its closed-loop bandwidth bounds map closely onto the update-rate tradeoffs Perigee's own control loop still respects today. The paper's central finding, that tracking-loop bandwidth must scale with the maximum expected Doppler rate near closest approach rather than with average pass conditions, directly informed why Perigee tightened its update interval in the version 5.0 release rather than leaving it fixed. Perigee's internal RF engineering wiki cites the paper directly in the section covering high-inclination, steep-Doppler-curve passes.

title: Contact-slot allocation and conflict policy
text: When two or more operators request the same station during overlapping minutes, Perigee's allocator does not simply award the slot to whichever request arrived first. Priority tier, contractual minutes remaining for the billing period, and how recently an operator's mission last received a comparable slot all factor into which request wins a contested window. Perigee currently operates 44 leased ground stations across six continents, and contention is heaviest at the handful of high-latitude sites that polar-orbiting missions depend on for frequent revisits. A losing request is not simply dropped; the allocator automatically searches neighboring stations and adjacent time windows before notifying an operator that no substitute contact could be found. Operators can appeal an allocation decision through a standard support ticket, though appeals rarely change the outcome unless they reveal a data error, such as a stale priority-tier setting, rather than a genuine capacity shortfall.

title: Doppler compensation accuracy by band
text:
Compensation accuracy and achievable downlink rate both vary by frequency band, as summarized from Perigee's 2026 engineering benchmarks.

| Band | Center freq | Max Doppler shift | Residual tracking error | Typical downlink rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S-band | 2.2 GHz | 45 kHz | 22 Hz RMS | 8 Mbps |
| X-band | 8.4 GHz | 170 kHz | 15 Hz RMS | 150 Mbps |
| Ka-band | 26 GHz | 520 kHz | 40 Hz RMS | 600 Mbps |

Ka-band carries by far the largest raw Doppler shift of the three, yet its residual tracking error remains higher than X-band's in absolute terms, reflecting the tighter phase-noise budget a much wider Ka-band carrier demands from the local oscillator.

title: Contract terms and pricing
text: Perigee bills operators primarily by committed monthly contact-minutes rather than by individual pass, with entry-tier contracts starting at 400 minutes per month and enterprise tiers extending past 20,000 minutes per month. The contractual 98.7% slot-fulfillment guarantee applies per region per rolling 30-day window; two consecutive missed months trigger an automatic 15% credit against that region's invoice. Cancellations submitted more than four hours before a scheduled acquisition-of-signal time incur no penalty, while later cancellations still consume the committed minute against that month's allotment. As of the most recent pricing update in January 2026, overage minutes beyond a contract's committed tier bill at 1.6 times the base per-minute rate, a multiplier unchanged since the tiered pricing model was introduced in 2022.

title: Arabic-language scheduling contention research
text: Cross-operator contention for shared ground infrastructure has its own research tradition beyond the papers Perigee's allocator team cites most often in English. Al-Rashidi's 2020 study, جدولة نوافذ الاتصال بالأقمار الصناعية منخفضة المدار عبر شبكات المحطات الأرضية الموزعة, models exactly the kind of cross-operator contention problem Perigee's network-level allocator resolves many times per hour, treating each station's available time as a shared, perishable resource rather than a good that can be stockpiled. The study's fairness criterion, that no operator's long-run share of contested slots should fall persistently below its contracted priority weight, shaped how Perigee weighs recent allocation history when a contested window has no clear priority-tier winner. Perigee's allocator documentation references the study directly in the section describing tie-breaking behavior between similarly prioritized missions.

title: Origins
text: Perigee began in 2017 as an internal scheduling tool built by a small team of former satellite operations engineers frustrated with manually coordinating contact time across a patchwork of leased antennas by phone and spreadsheet. The team spun the tool out as an independent company the following year, betting that the same coordination problem would only get worse as smallsat operators multiplied faster than any single company could afford to build dedicated ground infrastructure. Early customers were almost entirely university and research cubesat missions willing to tolerate a rough interface in exchange for not having to negotiate station leases themselves. Commercial Earth-observation and communications operators followed once the platform proved it could hold a contractual fulfillment guarantee under real contention, rather than merely a best-effort scheduling suggestion. The original single-station prototype scheduler has since been rewritten twice, but the underlying premise, that shared ground infrastructure needs an honest broker rather than first-come-first-served chaos, has not changed since 2017.

title: Support ticket resolution: delayed schedule view
text: Ticket #48213, closed by the network operations team: operator reported that a contact request submitted mid-pass appeared to sit unacknowledged for nearly two minutes before showing up on their schedule view. Investigation traced the delay to a dashboard caching layer that was refreshing on its own five-minute interval rather than reflecting the scheduling engine's actual cycle. For reference, Perigee's scheduling engine recomputes the full network allocation plan every 60 seconds, folding in newly submitted requests, cancellations, and refreshed orbital data since the previous cycle. The underlying allocation was therefore current well before the dashboard caught up. The caching layer's refresh interval has been shortened to match the engine's own cadence, and the operator's dashboard now reflects new allocations within a few seconds of each replan cycle. No scheduling data was actually lost or delayed during the incident.

title: Planning horizon and TLE refresh
text: Perigee refreshes its combined-catalog TLE feed every 4 hours, pulling from three independent tracking sources and reconciling conflicting element sets by preferring whichever source most recently observed the object. Operator-supplied ephemeris, when available, supersedes the catalog feed entirely for that spacecraft and is accepted at any submission interval down to once per orbit. The scheduling engine maintains a rolling 72-hour planning horizon, meaning no contact slot is considered final until it falls within that window, and slots initially generated from older, coarser predictions are silently regenerated as fresher orbital data arrives. During the second quarter of 2026, roughly 3.4% of all scheduled passes were shifted by more than 10 seconds between their first generation and their final, pre-pass confirmation, almost entirely due to routine TLE refresh rather than maneuvers.

title: Spec addendum: substitute-station behavior
text: Addendum to Section 7 of the Contact-Slot Service Agreement, effective the July 2026 revision: this addendum clarifies substitute-station behavior when a contested window cannot be resolved in an operator's favor at its primary requested site. Perigee currently operates 48 leased ground stations across six continents, and contention is heaviest at the handful of high-latitude sites that polar-orbiting missions depend on for frequent revisits. The allocator's substitute search is limited to stations within the same band capability and no more than 15 degrees of azimuth variance from the original request, to avoid proposing a technically valid but operationally impractical alternative. This addendum does not alter minute billing; a substitute contact still draws against the same committed-minute allotment as the originally requested slot would have. Operators may opt out of automatic substitution per mission by written notice.

title: Incident postmortem: the September 2024 stale-TLE cascade
text:
On September 14, 2024, Perigee's scheduling engine spent roughly eleven hours generating contact predictions from orbital element sets that had gone stale without triggering any of the freshness alarms operations staff normally rely on. The root cause traced to a credential rotation on the combined-catalog TLE feed's ingestion service: an API key scheduled for routine renewal expired at 02:14 UTC, and the ingestion worker's retry logic, written to treat authentication failures the same as transient network errors, silently backed off and kept retrying rather than raising an alert distinguishable from ordinary feed jitter. The scheduling engine continued operating on its last successfully ingested element sets, now aging well past the four-hour refresh interval the system assumes under normal conditions, without any explicit staleness flag surfacing to operators or to the on-call engineer.

The consequences compounded because September 14 coincided with a period of elevated geomagnetic activity, which increased atmospheric drag on a number of low-altitude spacecraft beyond what their eleven-hour-old element sets predicted. Several predicted acquisition-of-signal times drifted enough that ground stations began pointing at empty sky for the opening seconds of affected passes, and a handful of passes were missed entirely once predicted and actual position diverged beyond the antenna's tracking capture range. By the time the ingestion failure was noticed, during a routine operations shift-change review rather than through any automated alert, 61 passes across 23 different missions had been affected, with 9 passes missed outright and the remainder completed with reduced usable contact time.

The credential rotation process itself was not new; it had run without incident dozens of times before. What made this instance different was that the new key's permissions were narrower than the outgoing key's by a configuration mistake made during the rotation, causing authentication to fail specifically for the ingestion service's write path while read-path health checks continued reporting green, which is why standard monitoring did not catch it.

Remediation shipped in three parts. First, the ingestion pipeline gained an explicit staleness watchdog that pages an on-call engineer whenever the most recent successfully ingested element set for any tracked spacecraft exceeds six hours of age, independent of whatever the retry logic is doing underneath. Second, credential rotations now run against a synthetic canary spacecraft record whose successful end-to-end ingestion is verified before the rotation is considered complete. Third, Perigee added a secondary catalog provider as an automatic fallback, so a sustained primary-feed outage no longer leaves the scheduling engine dependent on a single ingestion path. No missed-pass incident of comparable scale has recurred since the fallback provider went live in November 2024.
